← all tutorials

Raft Consensus Algorithm — interactive 📖 RAFT.md 📄 raft.py

📖 Full guide  ·  📄 Ground-truth source — every panel below recomputes the same model in JS.
Raft keeps a cluster agreeing on ONE ordered log via a single leader, randomized election timeouts, and majority-replicated commits. Run an election, replicate a log, repair a stale follower — then crash a node and watch the cluster re-elect a leader. The default 5-node election is recomputed live in JS from the identical seeded timeouts as raft.py Section A / GOLD CHECK.

① Leader election — randomized timeouts → RequestVote → majority

What to do: press Run election. Every node starts a FOLLOWER with a random timeout (the pinned raft.py seed-2024 values: 270, 196, 298, 227, 201 ms). The first timer to fire becomes a CANDIDATE, bumps the term, votes for itself, and requests votes. First to 3 wins. Click any node to crash/recover it, then re-run — a crashed node neither votes nor is elected.
N = 5, majority = floor(5/2)+1 = 3
follower candidate leader crashed (click node) election timeout
In words: any two majorities of 5 share ≥1 node, and that node votes for only ONE candidate per term — so at most one leader per term (election safety). Randomized timeouts make two candidates firing simultaneously (a split vote) unlikely; it just wastes a term.

② Log replication — AppendEntries → majority ack → commit

What this shows: leader n1 has log [x1,x2,x3] (term 1). It pushes AppendEntries to the 4 followers. A follower ACKs only if log[prevLogIndex].term == prevLogTerm. n2 is stale (missing x3) — watch the leader decrement nextIndex and re-send. Once a majority (3) has an entry it is committed.
leader = n1 (term 1), target entry = 1:x3
committed log entry stale / to-repair ACK  REJECT
The log-matching property: if a follower accepts AppendEntries(prevLogIndex=k, prevLogTerm=t), its log matches the leader's up to index k. The leader never overwrites its own log — it only appends; followers may be overwritten.

③ Log repair — overwrite conflicting entries from a dead leader

What this shows: a new leader (term 2) meets a follower carrying uncommitted entries d,e from an old dead leader (term 3) that never replicated them. Raft walks nextIndex down until it finds ONE matching (index,term), then truncates and overwrites everything after it. Step through it.
leader n0: 1:a, 1:b, 2:c  |  follower n3: 1:a, 3:d, 3:e
The conflicting d,e were uncommitted, so discarding them is safe — they could never have been committed, because the election restriction stops a candidate carrying the committed a,b from losing to one carrying only d,e. Committed data is never lost.

④ Gold check — the full pipeline, recomputed live in JS

What this proves: the page re-runs election + replication in your browser on the same seed-2024 timeouts and the same initial logs as raft.py Section A + GOLD CHECK. The green check: OK below confirms the JS numbers match the .py exactly.
check: … JS election + replication match raft.py GOLD CHECK (winner=n1, term=1, votes=5, commit_index=3, committed log = 1:x1,1:x2,1:x3).
